European Directorate for the Quality of Medicines & HealthCare (EDQM):

The European Directorate for the Quality of Medicines & HealthCare (EDQM) is a directorate within the Council of Europe, responsible for establishing quality standards for medicines and healthcare. The EDQM operates under the European Pharmacopoeia Convention and provides a range of services, including the establishment of legal and scientific standards for medicines, the licensing of medical products, and the certification of reference materials and laboratories.

The main functions of the EDQM include:

1. The elaboration and publication of quality standards for medicines, including the European Pharmacopoeia, which contains legally binding quality standards for the manufacture and control of medicinal products.

2. The certification of substances and reference materials used in the production of medicines, to ensure their quality and safety.

3. The inspection and certification of laboratories involved in the testing of medicines and medical devices.

4. The provision of technical assistance and training to member states on issues related to quality control of medicines.

5. The coordination of a network of national official control laboratories (OMCLs) to ensure that medicines placed on the market comply with established quality standards.

The work of the EDQM contributes to ensuring the safety and quality of medicines used by patients across Europe and beyond.
Heads of Medicines Agencies (HMA):

Heads of Medicines Agencies (HMA) is a network of the heads of the competent authorities responsible for the regulation of medicinal products for human and veterinary use in the European Economic Area (EEA). The HMA operates as a platform for cooperation and coordination among the regulatory bodies, facilitating the exchange of information and best practices related to the authorization, supervision, and pharmacovigilance of medicines in the EEA.

The HMA network comprises of the heads of the national regulatory agencies of the 31 EEA countries, including all 27 EU member states and Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway. The HMA is committed to ensuring the highest standards of human and animal health by promoting cooperation among the member states and the European Medicines Agency (EMA).

The objectives of the HMA include:

1. To promote effective, efficient, and timely authorization of medicinal products in the EEA.

2. To optimize the supervision of the safety, quality, and efficacy of medicinal products authorized in the EEA.

3. To facilitate the exchange of information and best practices among the regulatory bodies in the EEA.

4. To provide effective support to the management of public health risks related to medicinal products.

5. To advocate for the regulatory interests of the EEA at the international level.

The HMA plays a significant role in the regulatory landscape of the EEA, promoting an integrated and harmonized approach to the regulation of medicinal products, and safeguarding the public health interests of the citizens of the EEA.
About the activities of the European Commission (EC) (literature, pdf, footnote)

The European Commission (EC) is the executive body of the European Union (EU), responsible for proposing and implementing EU policies, managing the budget, and enforcing EU law. The EC is composed of one representative from each EU member state, appointed by the national government, and is headed by the President of the European Commission, who is elected by the European Council.

The activities of the EC cover a wide range of areas, including:

1. Promoting the economic and social development of the EU through the implementation of policies and programs, such as the European Green Deal, the Digital Single Market, and the European Semester.

2. Ensuring the rule of law and the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms in the EU, by enforcing EU law and monitoring and reporting on the implementation of EU policies.

3. Negotiating and implementing international agreements and partnerships on behalf of the EU, both with other countries and with international organizations, such as the United Nations and the World Trade Organization.

4. Ensuring the safety and security of EU citizens through policies and initiatives in areas such as border control, counter-terrorism, and cybersecurity.

5. Managing the EU budget and financial programs, such as the European Regional Development Fund and the Horizon Europe research program.

6. Coordinating and supporting the work of the EU member states in areas such as foreign policy, health, and education.

The EC works closely with other EU institutions, including the European Parliament and the Council of the EU, as well as with national governments and stakeholders from civil society, industry, and academia. Its activities are guided by the principles of democracy, transparency, accountability, and the pursuit of the common good of the EU and its citizens.

References:
- "The European Commission: An Overview (2021)" - European Parliament - https://www.europarl.europa.eu/factsheets/en/sheet/9/the-european-commission-an-overview
- "About the European Commission (2021)" - European Commission - https://ec.europa.eu/info/about-european-commission_en
about the National Competent Authorities (NCAs) (literature, pdf, footnote)

National Competent Authorities (NCAs) are the regulatory bodies responsible for the implementation and enforcement of medicinal product regulations in individual European Union member states. NCAs play a crucial role in ensuring the safety, efficacy, and quality of medicinal products marketed in the EU market.

NCAs are responsible for a wide range of activities, including:

1. The evaluation and supervision of clinical trials of medicinal products conducted in their respective countries.

2. The authorization and monitoring of the marketing of medicinal products within their respective jurisdictions.

3. The surveillance of the safety and efficacy of medicinal products placed on the market, including the collection and analysis of adverse drug reaction reports.

4. The supervision of the manufacturing, importing, and distribution of medicinal products.

5. The provision of scientific and technical expertise to support other regulatory authorities in the EU.

6. The enforcement of compliance with applicable medicinal product regulations, and the imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

NCAs work closely with other regulatory bodies, including the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and the Heads of Medicines Agencies (HMA), to ensure the safety and efficacy of medicinal products marketed in the EU. They also collaborate with other stakeholders, including healthcare professionals, patients, and industry representatives, to improve the regulatory framework for medicinal products.

NCAs are appointed by each individual EU member state and are responsible for implementing the relevant EU regulations within their respective jurisdictions. They operate under the guidance of the EMA and are subject to its oversight and inspection.

References:
- "National Competent Authorities for Medicinal Products (2021)" - European Medicines Agency - https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/partners-networks/european-network-clinical-trial-supply-national-competent-authorities/national-competent-authorities-medicinal-products
- "Medicines regulatory authorities: functions and organisation (2019)" - European Commission - https://ec.europa.eu/health/sites/default/files/files/eg_documents/bf_good_brochure_v1_en.pdf
about the Directive 2001/83/EC on the Community code relating to medicinal products for human use (literature, pdf, foot-note)

Directive 2001/83/EC is a European Union (EU) legislative act that sets out the legal framework for the regulation of medicinal products for human use within the EU. The directive lays down rules governing the development, manufacture, distribution, and marketing of medicinal products, with the aim of ensuring their quality, safety, and efficacy.

Key provisions of Directive 2001/83/EC include:

1. Establishing a centralized system for the authorization of medicinal products, allowing for the mutual recognition of marketing authorizations among EU member states.

2. Requiring that medicinal products be classified as prescription-only or non-prescription, based on their safety and efficacy profile.

3. Requiring the submission of a dossier of scientific and technical data to support the marketing authorization application of medicinal products.

4. Setting out the requirements for the labeling and packaging of medicinal products, including the provision of clear and concise information for patients.

5. Requiring the reporting of adverse reactions associated with medicinal products, and the monitoring of their safety and efficacy following marketing authorization.

6. Setting out the requirements for inspections and audits of manufacturing sites and the imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

Directive 2001/83/EC has been revised and updated several times, most notably through Directive 2004/27/EC and Directive 2010/84/EU. The directive has had a significant impact on the regulation of medicinal products within the EU, ensuring a high level of protection for EU citizens while also facilitating the development and marketing of innovative medicinal products.

Reference:
- "Directive 2001/83/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 6 November 2001 on the Community code relating to medicinal products for human use (2010 Consolidated Version)" - European Commission - https://ec.europa.eu/health/sites/default/files/files/eudralex/vol-1/dir_2001_83_consol_2012/dir_2001_83_cons_2012_en.pdf
about the Directive 2001/82/EC on the Community code relating to veterinary medicinal products (literature, pdf, foot-note)

Directive 2001/82/EC is a European Union (EU) legislative act that sets out the legal framework for the regulation of veterinary medicinal products within the EU. The directive lays down rules governing the development, manufacture, distribution, and marketing of veterinary medicinal products, with the aim of ensuring their quality, safety, and efficacy.

Key provisions of Directive 2001/82/EC include:

1. Establishing a centralized or decentralized system for the authorization of veterinary medicinal products, depending on the intended use and scope of the product.

2. Requiring that veterinary medicinal products be classified as prescription-only or non-prescription, based on their safety and efficacy profile.

3. Requiring the submission of a dossier of scientific and technical data to support the marketing authorization application of veterinary medicinal products.

4. Setting out the requirements for the labeling and packaging of veterinary medicinal products, including the provision of clear and concise information for users, including farmers, veterinarians, and pet owners.

5. Requiring the reporting of adverse reactions associated with veterinary medicinal products, and the monitoring of their safety and efficacy following marketing authorization.

6. Setting out the requirements for inspections and audits of manufacturing sites and the imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

Directive 2001/82/EC has been revised and updated several times, most notably through Directive 2004/28/EC and Directive 2010/63/EU. The directive has had a significant impact on the regulation of veterinary medicinal products within the EU, ensuring a high level of protection for animals while also facilitating the development and marketing of innovative veterinary medicinal products.

Reference:
- "Directive 2001/82/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 6 November 2001 on the Community code relating to veterinary medicinal products" - European Commission - https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32001L0082.
about the Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 on the authorization and supervision of medicinal products for human and veterinary use (literature, pdf, foot-note)

Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 is a European Union (EU) legislative act that sets out the legal framework for the authorization, supervision, and pharmacovigilance of medicinal products for human and veterinary use within the EU. The regulation establishes a centralized procedure for the authorization of medicinal products and a harmonized approach to their supervision and post-marketing surveillance.

Key provisions of Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 include:

1. Establishing a centralized system for the authorization of medicinal products, allowing for the mutual recognition of marketing authorizations among EU member states.

2. Granting the European Medicines Agency (EMA) responsibility for the scientific evaluation of marketing authorization applications for medicinal products.

3. Providing for the coordination and cooperation between the EMA and national competent authorities (NCAs) in the evaluation and supervision of medicinal products.

4. Establishing a system for the monitoring and reporting of adverse drug reactions (pharmacovigilance) associated with medicinal products.

5. Setting out the requirements for inspections and audits of manufacturing sites and the imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

6. Providing for the granting of conditional marketing authorizations for medicinal products that address unmet medical needs or rare diseases.

Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 has been revised and updated several times, most notably through Regulation (EU) 2019/6, which entered into force in January 2019. The regulation has had a significant impact on the regulation of medicinal products within the EU, ensuring a high level of protection for EU citizens while also facilitating the development and marketing of innovative medicinal products.

Reference:
- "Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 31 March 2004 laying down Community procedures for the authorisation and supervision of medicinal products for human and veterinary use and establishing a European Medicines Agency" - European Commission - https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32004R0726
about the  Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 on clinical trials on medicinal products for human use. (literature, pdf, foot-note)

Regulation (EU) No 536/2014, commonly known as the Clinical Trials Regulation, is a European Union (EU) regulation that sets out the legal framework for conducting clinical trials on medicinal products for human use within the EU. The regulation replaces the previous Clinical Trials Directive 2001/20/EC and aims to simplify and harmonize the procedures for conducting clinical trials across the EU.

Key provisions of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 include:

1. The establishment of a centralized EU portal and database for the submission and maintenance of clinical trial applications and related information.

2. The requirement for each EU member state to designate a national competent authority responsible for the assessment and authorization of clinical trials within their respective jurisdictions.

3. The provision for the recognition of a single opinion on the clinical trial application across all EU member states, known as the EU-wide single assessment.

4. The introduction of simplified procedures for clinical trials that meet certain criteria, such as those conducted on low-risk medicinal products or in vulnerable populations.

5. The requirement for transparency and public access to clinical trial information, including the publication of summary results and lay summaries of clinical trial results.

6. The strengthening of the protection and rights of clinical trial participants.

Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 is scheduled to come into force once the necessary technical infrastructure for the EU portal and database is fully operational, currently expected in 2022. The regulation is expected to improve the efficiency and quality of clinical trials conducted in the EU, while also ensuring the protection of participants and the reliability of clinical trial results.

Reference:
- "Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 April 2014 on clinical trials on medicinal products for human use, and repealing Directive 2001/20/EC" - European Commission - https://ec.europa.eu/health/sites/default/files/files/eudralex/vol-1/reg_2014_536/reg_2014_536_en.pdf
about the European Medicines Agency (EMA). (literature, pdf, foot-note)

The European Medicines Agency (EMA) is a decentralized agency of the European Union (EU) responsible for the scientific evaluation, supervision, and safety monitoring of medicinal products for human and veterinary use within the EU. The EMA was established in 1995 and is located in Amsterdam, the Netherlands.

Key responsibilities of the EMA include:

1. Providing scientific advice and guidance to companies during the development and authorization of medicinal products for human and veterinary use.

2. Reviewing and evaluating marketing authorization applications for medicinal products for human and veterinary use, in collaboration with national regulatory authorities.

3. Monitoring and supervising the safety and efficacy of medicinal products following marketing authorization, including the collection and assessment of reports on adverse drug reactions.

4. Facilitating and coordinating EU-wide initiatives to promote the development of innovative and safe medicinal products.

5. Providing access to information on medicinal products to healthcare professionals, patients, and the public.

6. Providing support and coordinating the evaluation of applications for orphan designations, which provide incentives for the development of medicinal products for rare diseases.
